text: The surgeon addresses adhesions between the sigmoid colon and rectum, freeing them to restore normal anatomy. The sigmoid colon appears floppy and redundant. A tattoo mark is visible distal to the lipoma, which occupies most of the colonic lumen.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect through the thin, floppy mesentery of the sigmoid colon, advancing towards the proximal resection margin. Four 8mm robotic ports are utilized for the procedure, anticipating favorable anatomy for transanal extraction and intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon finishes the dissection at the rectosigmoid junction using a vessel sealer, carefully cutting across the tissue to minimize bleeding and avoid burnt tissue during the anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the proximal colon and cleans off surrounding fat to prepare for a purse string suture. A thoracic ring forcep is used to assist in extracting the specimen, revealing the extruded lipoma as it is pulled out without excessive force on the rectum.

The surgeon uses two points of retraction to position the anvil stationary and pulls the lips of the colon over it. A purse string suture is applied around the rectum, starting on the right side and overlapping the initial point to ensure a tight cinch. The surgeon prepares to deploy a 33mm EEA stapler, aiming to insert the spike through the center of the purse string suture.

The surgeon uses a rigid proctoscope to perform a leak test on the anastomosis, ensuring the scope reaches above the anastomotic site. The anastomosis is fired from below, completing the connection between the two bowel segments.
